blogs.subtitle: Thoughts living abroad, software development, and personal growth.

cta.text: I enjoy working on meaningful projects and continuously improving both my technical and architectural skills. If you think we could work well together, feel free to reach out.

about.pageDescription: Learn more about Arhan Konuksal — background, skills, and approach to software development.
about.bio1: I'm Arhan Konuksal, a full-stack developer currently studying Applied Computer Science & AI in Antwerp, Belgium.
about.bio2: I specialize in building modern web applications using the PERN stack (PostgreSQL, Express, React, Node.js). My focus is on clean architecture, maintainable code, and creating solutions that solve real problems.
about.bio3: When I'm not coding, I'm usually exploring new technologies, learning about system design, or working on side projects that challenge me to grow as a developer.

livingAbroadAsCypriot.title: Living Abroad as a Cypriot
livingAbroadAsCypriot.subtitle: What I learned stepping out of my comfort zone and building a new life in Belgium.

content.text: Growing up in Cyprus and then going abroad for university wasn't just an educational decision for me. It was actually stepping into an entirely different life. When I first arrived in Belgium, what I noticed was that this place was a different world — not just physically, but mentally as well.

In Cyprus, everything was more familiar, more comfortable, and slower. Here, everything is more structured, faster, and more individual. Nobody knows you, nobody guides you. You truly have to carve your own path. It's tough at first, but at the same time incredibly empowering.
Stepping Out of the Comfort Zone

The biggest impact of living abroad is that it pulls you out of your comfort zone. You're away from your family, your usual routine is gone, and you have to handle even the simplest things on your own. But that's exactly why you grow. Your self confidence increases and the way you solve problems changes.
A Different Perspective

Meeting people from different countries and seeing how they think seriously shifts your perspective. My view of technology and the business world became much clearer here. I started thinking more globally and gained a better understanding of where my work truly stands.
Getting to Know Yourself

Perhaps most importantly, living abroad helps you get to know yourself. You discover what you want, what you can endure, and what you truly care about. This process isn't always easy, but it's definitely worth it.
For me, living abroad isn't just an educational journey — it's a period where I've been growing as a person and shaping my life. And seeing where this journey takes me is truly exciting.

journeyCyprusToBelgium.title: From Cyprus to Belgium: Building My Own Path
journeyCyprusToBelgium.subtitle: What I learned while leaving a small island and trying to find my place in the world of software.

content.text: Growing up in Cyprus gave me a lot. I was raised in a smaller, more familiar, and more comfortable environment. But at some point I realized: if I truly wanted to grow, I needed to step outside of where I was. Not just physically, but mentally as well.

My interest in software started early, but it took time before I started taking it seriously. At first, I was mostly learning through trial and error. I built simple projects, tried different things, but didn't really know what I was doing. Over time I realized: just writing code isn't enough — you need to understand why you're doing what you're doing.
The First Turning Point

The most important turning point in my self taught journey was when I started seeing software as a tool. Before, I was just focused on making things work. But then I realized the real goal is solving problems. Code is just a means to that end.
With this awareness, my approach to projects changed. I stopped asking only 'does it work?' and started asking 'is it correct?', 'is it maintainable?', and 'is it scalable?'.
Arriving in Belgium

Moving to Belgium was one of the most important steps in this journey. A new country, a new system, and a completely different lifestyle. Everything felt foreign at first, but it made me more disciplined and independent.

While studying here, I also focused more on developing myself technically. I didn't just want to pass courses — I wanted to truly learn. So I started taking projects more seriously and raised my own standards.
Learning by Doing

For me, the most effective way to learn has always been by doing. I started with small projects, then moved on to more complex systems. I made mistakes, sometimes started over, but each time I developed a better understanding.

Working on full-stack development in particular gave me the opportunity to see the entire system. Understanding the relationship between frontend, backend, and database completely changed my perspective.
A Shift in Perspective
Over time, my view of software became more professional. It's no longer just about being technically good — solving the right problems and creating real value matters more to me now.
At the same time, my interest in the business side grew during this process. I realized that software isn't just a technical field — it's directly connected to business, strategy, and people.
Today and Beyond
Today I still see myself at the beginning of the road, but I know what I'm doing much better now. I can see more clearly which areas I need to improve in, how I work, and what I'm aiming for.
This journey from Cyprus to Belgium wasn't just a geographical change — it was a transformation in mindset. And I'm only just getting started.
